stories.title: A Trip to the Museum
stories.story:
Our class had been looking forward to the museum trip for weeks. When we finally arrived, I was amazed by how enormous the building was — it seemed to stretch on forever, with tall stone pillars at the entrance.

Our first stop was the ancient Egypt exhibit, where I stood face to face with a real mummy that was over three thousand years old. Our guide explained how ancient Egyptians believed in an afterlife, and how they carefully preserved bodies so they could continue living in the next world. I had read about this in books, but seeing it in person felt completely different — almost like traveling back in time.

The most unforgettable part of the trip, though, was the dinosaur skeleton hall. A massive Tyrannosaurus Rex skeleton towered above us, its jaw filled with teeth as long as my hand. Our guide explained that scientists had spent over two years carefully assembling the bones after discovering them buried in the desert.

On the bus ride home, I couldn't stop talking about everything I had seen. My friend Malak said the trip made her want to become an archaeologist one day, digging up ancient treasures herself. I realized that museums aren't just about looking at old objects — they're about understanding the incredible history that came before us.

stories.title: The Language Exchange
stories.story:
When a student from Japan named Kenji joined our class for a month-long exchange program, I was assigned to be his partner. I was excited but also nervous — I had never really talked to someone who spoke a completely different language before.

On his first day, Kenji spoke very little English, and I knew absolutely no Japanese. We communicated mostly through drawings and hand gestures, which often led to funny misunderstandings. Once, I tried to explain "homework," and he thought I was talking about a house made of wood!

Despite the language barrier, Kenji and I found things we both loved — especially football and a card game we both somehow knew. Every day, he taught me a few Japanese words, and I taught him English phrases in return. Slowly, our conversations became easier and easier.

By the end of the month, Kenji could hold a full conversation in English, and I had learned enough Japanese to say much more than "hello." When he left to go back home, we promised to keep messaging each other. I learned that even without sharing a language at first, friendship can find a way to communicate.
